items.q: How does a population of organisms become better adapted to its environment over time?
items.correct: Individuals with advantageous heritable variations survive and reproduce more; their alleles increase in frequency over generations.
items.misconception: Individual organisms change during their lifetime in response to their environment and pass the changes on to offspring.
items.random_wrong: Organisms decide what features they need and grow them by force of will.
items.why: The Lamarckian 'inheritance of acquired characteristics' is intuitive — students think exercise-built muscles or learned skills get passed to children.

items.correct: No — humans and chimpanzees share a common ancestor; both have evolved from it.
items.misconception: Humans evolved directly from chimpanzees, which is why chimps still exist as a less-evolved version.
items.random_wrong: Humans evolved from monkeys but chimps evolved from gorillas.
items.why: The phrase 'descended from apes' and linear evolution diagrams encourage 'A turned into B' thinking, missing the branching tree model.

items.q: What is produced in anaerobic respiration in human muscle cells?
items.correct: Lactic acid (lactate) and a small amount of ATP — no CO2 or water in this pathway.
items.misconception: Anaerobic respiration produces ethanol and CO2 in humans.
items.random_wrong: Anaerobic respiration produces oxygen as a waste product.
items.why: Students confuse anaerobic respiration in yeast (alcohol + CO2) with the human form (lactic acid).

items.q: Do individual animals evolve during their own lifetime?
items.correct: No — individuals do not evolve; populations change over generations as advantageous heritable traits become more common.
items.misconception: An individual animal evolves to suit its environment within its lifetime.
items.random_wrong: Animals decide to evolve the features they want.
items.why: Students apply evolution to individuals, but it is populations that evolve through differential survival and reproduction over many generations.

items.q: Is natural selection a random process?
items.correct: No — variation arises randomly (by mutation), but selection is not random: the best-suited individuals survive and reproduce more.
items.misconception: Natural selection is completely random, like luck.
items.random_wrong: Natural selection means organisms change their genes deliberately.
items.why: Students call the whole process random, but while mutation is random, the survival step is non-random, favouring advantageous traits.

items.q: Which of these is the correct order for natural selection?
items.correct: Variation → competition → survival of the fittest → reproduction
items.why: Correct answer: Variation → competition → survival of the fittest → reproduction. “Reproduction → variation → competition → survival” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What did Darwin notice about Galapagos finch beaks?
items.correct: They were shaped to match the food on each island
items.random_wrong: They changed colour with the seasons
items.why: The answer examiners want is: They were shaped to match the food on each island. “They were all identical” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Darwin publish 'On the Origin of Species'?
items.why: The real figure is 1859. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
